title: Strings — Working with Text in Python
key_insight: Strings store text. Python makes text manipulation easy with built-in methods for finding, splitting, joining, and formatting strings. Almost every real program works with strings.
body:
## Creating Strings

```python
name = 'Alice'          # single quotes
greeting = "Hello!"     # double quotes — both work
multiline = '''Line 1
Line 2
Line 3'''               # triple quotes for multiple lines
```

## String Operations

```python
first = 'Hello'
last  = 'World'

# Concatenation (joining strings)
full = first + ' ' + last
print(full)   # Hello World

# Repetition
line = '-' * 20
print(line)  # --------------------

# Length
print(len(full))  # 11
```

## Indexing and Slicing

```python
text = 'Python'
# Index:  0 1 2 3 4 5
# From end: -6 -5 -4 -3 -2 -1

print(text[0])    # 'P'  (first character)
print(text[-1])   # 'n'  (last character)
print(text[0:3])  # 'Pyt' (index 0 up to, not including, 3)
print(text[2:])   # 'thon' (from index 2 to end)
print(text[::-1]) # 'nohtyP' (reversed)
```

## Essential String Methods

```python
sentence = '  Hello, World!  '

print(sentence.strip())        # 'Hello, World!'  — remove whitespace
print(sentence.lower())        # '  hello, world!  '
print(sentence.upper())        # '  HELLO, WORLD!  '
print(sentence.replace('World', 'Python'))  # '  Hello, Python!  '
print(sentence.strip().split(', '))  # ['Hello', 'World!']

# Check methods
print('hello'.startswith('hel'))  # True
print('hello'.endswith('lo'))     # True
print('ello' in 'hello')          # True
print('hello'.count('l'))         # 2
```

## f-Strings — The Best Way to Format Strings

```python
name = 'Alice'
age  = 30
price = 19.99

# f-string: put f before the quote, use {} for variables
print(f'Hello, {name}! You are {age} years old.')
# Hello, Alice! You are 30 years old.

# Format numbers:
print(f'Price: £{price:.2f}')  # Price: £19.99
print(f'{age:05d}')            # 00030 (pad with zeros)
```
common_mistakes: Strings are immutable — you can't do `name[0] = 'B'`. Create a new string instead
Forgetting that split() returns a list, not a string — `'a,b,c'.split(',')` → `['a', 'b', 'c']`
mini_exercise: Given `s = 'Hello, World!'`, write expressions to: (1) get only 'World', (2) convert to uppercase, (3) check if 'World' is in the string, (4) replace 'World' with your name, (5) count how many 'l' characters there are.
real_world_usage: Every web form, user profile, error message, and file path in software is a string. Python string methods are used constantly: strip() removes whitespace from user input, split() parses CSV data, f-strings format output. Django templates use Python string formatting to generate HTML.
explain_back_prompt: What is string slicing and how does negative indexing work? What does split() return? What is an f-string and how is it different from string concatenation with `+`?

build_task.title: Build a username validator
build_task.context: String methods are perfect for input validation.
steps: Check: must start with a letter (use .isalpha() on first character)
Check: can only contain letters, numbers, underscores (use .isalnum() and check for underscore)
Check: must be lowercase (or auto-lowercase it with .lower())
Print 'Valid username' or specific error messages
build_task.expected_output: Username validator that accepts 'alice_99' and rejects '1user', 'ab', 'user name'.
build_task.starter_code:
username = input('Enter a username: ').strip()

# TODO: validate each rule with string methods
# Rules: 3-20 chars, starts with letter, only alphanum/underscore, lowercase
